lesson: L11 — Polygons: Angles, Diagonals & Regular Polygons
banner: Week 13 · Polygons — Angles, Diagonals & Regular Polygons
essential_question: If a triangle's angles always sum to 180° and a quadrilateral's always sum to 360°, what is the sum for ANY polygon — and is there one formula that works for every number of sides at once?
objective: Name a polygon by its number of sides, find the number of diagonals from one vertex and the interior angle sum using (n-2)×180°, and find each interior and exterior angle of a regular polygon.

content.prompt: A triangle's angles always add to 180°. A quadrilateral's always add to 360°. Before you learn a single new rule, guess out loud: does a pentagon's angle sum keep climbing by another 180°? What about a hexagon? Is there a pattern, or does every shape need its own separate rule?

agenda.title: I do — Name It, Split It, Sum It

content.heading: Diagonals from one vertex turn any polygon into triangles
bullets: Polygons are named by their number of sides: 3=triangle, 4=quadrilateral, 5=pentagon, 6=hexagon, 7=heptagon, 8=octagon, 9=nonagon, 10=decagon, 11=undecagon, 12=dodecagon — beyond that, just say 'n-gon' (a 15-gon, a 20-gon).

A diagonal connects two NON-adjacent vertices. From any ONE vertex of an n-sided polygon you can draw exactly n − 3 diagonals (you skip yourself and your two neighbors, who are already joined to you by a side).
Those n − 3 diagonals from one vertex split the whole polygon into exactly n − 2 triangles — and since every triangle's angles sum to 180°, the polygon's interior angle sum is (n − 2) × 180°.
A REGULAR polygon has all sides congruent AND all angles congruent, so dividing the total by n gives each interior angle: (n − 2) × 180° ÷ n.

Every convex polygon's exterior angles (one per vertex) always add up to exactly 360°, no matter how many sides — so a regular polygon's each exterior angle is 360° ÷ n, and interior + exterior = 180° at every vertex.

agenda.title: Explore — Slide the Sides, Watch It All Update

content.heading: Drag the slider from 3 sides to 12 and read the live totals
content.note: On the sim, use the slider or the − / + buttons to change the number of sides from 3 to 12. Watch the diagonal count, the interior angle sum, and each interior angle update live. Stop at n = 4 — what do you notice about the interior angle? Then watch what happens to each interior angle as n climbs toward 12.

content.preview: Build session: hunt down or build real regular polygons and verify their diagonal counts and angle sums.

success_criteria.text: I can name a polygon by its number of sides and find the diagonals-from-one-vertex and triangle count
I can find a polygon's interior angle sum using (n-2)×180°
I can find each interior and exterior angle of a regular polygon, and find n from a given exterior angle

questions.explain: 9 sides = nonagon. A polygon always has the same number of angles as sides, so you can count either one.

questions.prompt: Drawing every diagonal from ONE vertex of an n-sided polygon splits it into how many triangles?
questions.explain: n-3 diagonals from one vertex leave n-2 triangles — that's exactly why the interior angle sum formula is (n-2)×180°.

questions.prompt: A regular hexagon (6 sides). How many diagonals can you draw from ONE vertex, and how many triangles does that split it into?
questions.answer: 3 diagonals from one vertex (6-3=3); those diagonals split the hexagon into 4 triangles (6-2=4).
questions.explain: n-3 gives diagonals from one vertex; n-2 gives the resulting triangle count — here n=6.

questions.prompt: On the grid, sketch a pentagon (5 sides) and draw every diagonal from ONE vertex. Label how many diagonals you drew and how many triangles they created.
questions.answer: A closed 5-sided figure with 2 diagonals drawn from one vertex (5-3=2), creating 3 triangles (5-2=3).
questions.explain: Rubric: a closed 5-sided figure (1 pt) + exactly 2 diagonals from one vertex correctly splitting it into 3 triangles (1 pt).

questions.prompt: A regular polygon has an exterior angle measuring 40° at every vertex. What is the polygon's name?
questions.explain: n = 360°÷40° = 9 sides — a nonagon. Every regular polygon's exterior angles always add to 360°, so dividing 360° by one exterior angle gives the number of sides.

questions.prompt: The sum of the interior angles of a polygon is 1440°. How many sides does it have, and what is the name of that polygon?
questions.explain: Divide both sides of (n-2)×180°=1440° by 180° to get n-2=8, so n=10 — a decagon.

questions.prompt: Exit: find one real polygon around you right now (a stop sign, a nut or bolt head, a floor tile, a piece of pasta) and name it by its number of sides. If it looks regular, what would each interior angle measure?
